hook: You already know that shapes can be moved, flipped, and resized — you've been doing it every time you arrange furniture in a room or zoom in on a photo. But geometry gives you precise tools to describe and predict exactly how a shape changes, and surprisingly, many of those changes preserve perfect congruence or similarity. What if a single set of rules could explain everything from kaleidoscope patterns to the tiles on your bathroom floor?
concept: This unit is about the ways shapes can move or change in a plane while following strict mathematical rules — the unifying idea is the concept of a *geometric transformation*, a function that maps every point of a figure to a new location, preserving specific properties such as distance, angle, or ratio.

topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to slide a figure any direction by a fixed distance using coordinate rules of the form $(x, y) \to (x+a, y+b)$, keeping its shape and size perfectly intact.
Master how to flip a figure across a line of reflection and find the exact coordinates of its mirror image on the coordinate plane.
Apply rotation rules to turn a figure a given number of degrees around a center point, and recognize how the coordinates transform for common angles like $90°$, $180°$, and $270°$.
You will learn to combine two or more transformations in sequence and determine the single net effect on a figure's position and orientation.
Apply dilations to scale a figure larger or smaller by a scale factor $k$ from a center point, producing a figure that is similar — but not necessarily congruent — to the original.
You will identify and describe the lines of symmetry and the order of rotational symmetry present in geometric figures and real-world objects.
Explore how repeated transformations of one or more shapes can tile an infinite plane without gaps or overlaps, and determine which regular polygons can tessellate.
